deficits.missing: Expert collapse / dead-expert is explained conceptually (rich-get-richer, aux-loss f_e computation) but there is no runtime diagnostic code to DETECT a dead expert during training — e.g. logging a per-expert token-count histogram, per-expert utilization fraction, or a routing-entropy signal to alert when one expert stops receiving tokens.
deficits.fix: Add a small `@torch.no_grad()` monitoring snippet that accumulates per-expert dispatch counts over a window and flags experts whose share falls below (1/E)/threshold, mirroring the TrainingMonitor pattern in 3.11; state the expected balanced value (~1/E per expert) as the sanity target.

deficits.missing: Verification is demonstrated ad hoc per component (round-trip in 2.1, gradcheck in 1.6/1.7/4.2, allclose-vs-reference in 2.3, ln(V) init loss in 2.7/3.17) but the book never consolidates these into a single 'how to structure a component test suite' reference — no pytest layout, no CI-style ordered checklist that a reader runs top-to-bottom before a real run.
deficits.fix: Add a short 'Component verification suite' section (or appendix box) that lists, in dependency order, the one-line assertion for each component the reader built — tokenizer round-trip, attention vs F.scaled_dot_product_attention, gradcheck on any custom Function, ln(V) init loss, resume bit-continuity, MoE balance — as a copy-pasteable pytest file with the expected numeric tolerance for each.

deficits.missing: Silent SHAPE/broadcast bugs (the class that trains but converges wrong, e.g. a (B,T) vs (B,1) mask that broadcasts silently, or a transposed reshape that mixes heads) get one good callout in 2.3 (softmax over key axis) but are not treated as a general failure class with a detection recipe.
deficits.fix: Add a paragraph on catching silent broadcasting/reshape bugs: assert exact shapes at each stage, verify attention weights sum to 1 over the key axis, and confirm no-future-leakage (triu(w,1)==0) — pointing out that these bugs pass 'not crashing' but show up as a loss that plateaus above the ln(V)/scaling-law expectation rather than as a crash.

summary: Verification/testing/debugging discipline is a REFERENCE-grade strength of this book. Every from-scratch component ships with a concrete correctness check and an expected numeric value: tokenizer decode(encode(s))==s adversarial round-trip plus a tiktoken parity assert (2.1); hand-rolled attention verified against F.scaled_dot_product_attention to atol=1e-5 with the softmax-axis bug and no-future-leakage checks called out (2.3); central-difference gradient_check returning ~1e-7 relative error (1.6) and torch.autograd.gradcheck on custom Functions (1.7); FlashAttention forward verified against a reference to ~1e-15 AND a finite-difference gradient check on the backward pass (4.2); the ln(V) initial-loss sanity check as the canonical 'is data/masking/loss wired correctly' probe (2.7, 3.17); param-count and resume-continuity checks (2.7). Diagnosis of loss spikes, NaNs, divergence, and fp16 attention-logit overflow is a full field manual in 3.11 (Adam-amplification worked example, war stories, a 6-step debugging checklist, a runnable stability_probe.py diagnostic, distributed NaN reduce, warning-threshold tables) reinforced by the recipe's troubleshooting table with expected perplexities (3.17). The profiling workflow to find the actual bottleneck is fully covered in 4.1 (roofline placement, torch.profiler with Chrome trace, Nsight Systems/Compute, MFU verdict). Library pointers are present throughout (gradcheck, F.scaled_dot_product_attention, torch.profiler, torch.utils.flop_counter, Nsight, OLMo, microsoft/mup). A competent reader can independently verify each component they build. Deficits are minor: no dead-expert runtime detector code, no consolidated component-test-suite reference, and silent shape/broadcast bugs are not treated as a named failure class with a detection recipe. No correctness bugs found in the verification content.
evidence: 2.1: 'Adversarial round-trip test ... verify decode(encode(s)) == s' with assert tok.decode(ids) == s over leading spaces, tabs, CJK, emoji; plus assert encode_ref(s, enc) == enc.encode(s) tiktoken parity.
2.3: 'assert torch.allclose(ours, ref, atol=1e-5)' against F.scaled_dot_product_attention; 'Always assert A.sum(dim=-1) is all-ones'; 'no future leakage: np.allclose(np.triu(w, k=1), 0.0)'.
1.6: gradient_check central-difference code prints '~1e-7' relative error; 'If your analytic gradient and the numerical one agree to ~6 decimal places, your backprop is almost certainly correct.'
1.7: torch.autograd.gradcheck example with 'Always run gradcheck on new Function implementations — it catches sign errors, missing factors, and wrongly accumulated terms.'
4.2: 'max abs error = ~1e-15: exact up to floating-point round-off' vs reference_attention, for both causal and non-causal; separate finite-difference gradient check on flash_attention_backward.

2.7: 'the loss starts near ln(65) ≈ 4.17 ... the cheapest sanity check that your data, masking, and loss are wired correctly'; four integration checks (param count ~10.6M, init loss, final loss, RoPE unit test).

3.11: worked Adam-amplification example (spike update 100x normal), warning-threshold tables, 6-step debugging checklist, runnable stability_probe.py with probe_activation_norms + compute_per_layer_grad_norms + diagnose(), check_for_nan_distributed all-reduce.

3.17: troubleshooting table ('Loss stuck near ln(vocab) -> targets not shifted ... decode a batch to real text; verify x=w[:-1], y=w[1:]'); expected perplexities ~4/~22/~14; HellaSwag random-floor expectation.

4.1: 'PROFILE one step (torch.profiler / nsys). Find the top time sinks'; PyTorch profiler with export_chrome_trace, Nsight Systems (nsys) and Compute (ncu) hierarchical roofline; MFU 40-55% healthy band, <30% signals a fixable bottleneck.
2.9: expert collapse explained as rich-get-richer with load-balancing aux loss and f_e hard-count fraction — but no runtime dead-expert detector code.
